chunks.title: Course Information
chunks.content:
Course Number: ISEN 414
Course Title: Total Quality Engineering
Section: 500
Time and Location: Lecture: 10:20-11:10 PM, Monday and Wednesday; ZACH 361
Lab: 3:00-5:30 PM, Friday; ZACH 344
Credit Hours: 3

chunks.title: Instructor Details
chunks.content:
Instructor: Dr. Zhijian (ZJ) Pei
Office: ETB 4006
Phone: 979-458-2334
E-Mail: zjpei@tamu.edu
Office Hours: 2:00-3:00 PM Monday and Wednesday

TA: Abhishek Jaiswal
Office: Cubicle #2, located across from ETB 4075.
Phone: 979-739-8279
E-Mail: abhishek.jai@tamu.edu
Office Hours: 4:00-5:00 PM Monday

chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content: Principles of total quality engineering; total quality management philosophy, engineering approaches for designing quality into products and processes; off-line experimentation methods for the robust design; emphasis on teamwork and continuous quality improvement.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Grade of C or better in STAT 211; junior or senior classification

chunks.title: Special Course Designation
chunks.content: NA

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Students will:
• Learn formulations, concepts, and analytical procedures for designing quality into products and processes
• Learn the connection between ISEN 414 and six-sigma program
• Learn fundamental principles of robust design and off-line experimentation techniques
• Learn to implement robust design tools in industrial applications
• Improve team working skill, data-collecting, and experiment planning capability

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
(Optional) Myers, R. H., Montgomery, D. C., and Anderson-Cook, C. M. 2016, Response Surface Methodology: Process and Product Optimization Using Designed Experiments, 4th edition, John Wiley & Sons.

Course Website: The course will use Canvas (https://canvas.tamu.edu/). Please log into the system using your NetID username and password and make sure you are able to access the course website. Please also check this website regularly, as it will be used to post announcements, quizzes, exams, lab assignments, and any other course material or information. Any information posted on the course website will be treated as if it is announced in the class and you are responsible to be aware of it.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Assignments/Quizzes: 25%
Exams: 50% (2 exams, 25% each)
Lab reports: 25%
Grades A for 90%-100%, B for 80%-89.99%, C for 70%–79.99%, D for 60%–69.99%, and F for <60%.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Missing an exam: If an exam is missed, you must have a written and authorized excuse that meets university excused absence requirements to qualify for a make-up exam. Wherever possible, students should inform the instructor before the exam. If not possible, the students must notify the instructor by the end of the second working day after missing an exam. Otherwise, they forfeit their rights to a make-up. Make-up exams will be given in accordance with University Rules (see Rule 7 at http://student-rules.tamu.edu/).

Missing a quiz: There is no make-up for missed quizzes. It is your responsibility to take the quizzes during the allocated time duration. Any student may drop two lowest quiz grades. If you miss a quiz due to an excused absence, your lowest grade (zero in this case) can be dropped, but no one can drop more than two grades.

Missing a lab: There is no make-up for labs. It is your responsibility to attend the lab and complete the assignment. However, if you miss them due to an excused absence, your lowest lab assignment grade (zero in this case) will be dropped and your average grade will be based on the remaining assignments.

chunks.title: Important notes regarding excused absence:
chunks.content:
The absence note must:
• Be in writing.
• Come from an authorized entity / authority.
• Clearly indicate the exact dates on which you are allowed to miss class/quiz/lab and state the reason. For example, just coming with a printed appointment from Student Health Services is not sufficient. According to student rules, “an absence for a non-acute medical service does not constitute an excused absence.” If you were absent due to a serious or contagious condition, you should provide the instructor with a medical confirmation from Student Health Services (be aware that the Student Health Service does not provide notes retroactively). The note must indicate that you are excused from attending class on a specific date and state the reason unless it violates FERPA rules: http://registrar.tamu.edu/Catalogs,-Policies-Procedures/FERPA/FERPA-Notice-to- Students.

The instructor reserves their right to verify documentation and decline what they do not deem as a legitimate university excused absence.

chunks.title: Re-grading Policy:
chunks.content: Any disagreements regarding a grade received on any graded material must be discussed within two business days of the return of the graded material. No grade will be changed beyond two business days.

chunks.title: Tentative Lab Schedule
chunks.content:
Note: Tentative guide for project progress. In-person labs include brief updates (slides due by noon before lab). Optional-attendance labs (no slides, meet independently): 2/13, 2/27, 3/27.
Legend: Project Deliverables, D1=identify gaps; D2=root causes; D3=solutions; D4=ROI.

| Week | Date | Topic | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1/16 | L1: Introductions; project overview; deliverables, timeline. | In-person; slides due noon 1/16. |
| 2 | 1/23 | L2: Team formation; partner org shortlist; outreach plan (D1). | In-person; slides due noon; suggested: org shortlist. |
| 3 | 1/30 | L3: Outreach updates; provisional partner org; draft two gaps (D1). | In-person; slides due noon; suggested: outreach progress. |
| 4 | 2/6 | L4: Confirm partner org; finalize scope, gaps, metrics (D1). | In-person; slides due noon; suggested: scope, gaps, metrics. |
| 5 | 2/13 | L5: Optional work session; current-state map, metrics (D1). | Optional; no slides; meet independently or visit partner organization. |
| 6 | 2/20 | L6: Root-cause tools selection; justify per gap (D2). | In-person; slides due noon; suggested: tool choices. |
| 7 | 2/27 | L7: Optional work session; draft fishbone/Pareto/5-Whys (D2). | Optional; no slides; meet independently; draft root-cause outputs. |
| 8 | 3/6 | L8: Validate causes; prioritize; begin solution concepts (D2 → D3). | In-person; slides due noon; suggested: prioritized root causes. |
| 9 | 3/9-13 | Spring Break | No lab |
| 10 | 3/20 | L9: Present solutions linked to causes; feasibility (D3). | In-person; slides due noon; suggested: solution matrix. |
| 11 | 3/27 | L10: Optional work session; build ROI estimate (D4). | Optional; no slides; meet independently; refine ROI assumptions. |
| 12 | 4/3 | L11: Presentation rehearsal; slide feedback; finalize storyline. | In-person; slides due noon; final presentations due 4/9 11:59pm CT. |
| 13 | 4/10 | L12: Final oral presentations - Session 1. | Slides due 4/9 11:59pm CT (submission). |
| 14 | 4/17 | L13: Final oral presentations - Session 2. | Suggested: incorporate feedback into individual report draft. |
| 15 | 4/24 | L14: Final oral presentations - Session 3. | Final individual report due 5/1 11:59pm CT. |

chunks.title: Attendance Policy
chunks.content:
The university views class attendance and participation as an individual student responsibility. Students are expected to attend class and to complete all assignments.

Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for information about excused absences, including definitions, and related documentation and timelines.

chunks.title: Makeup Work Policy
chunks.content:
Students will be excused from attending class on the day of a graded activity or when attendance contributes to a student's grade, for the reasons stated in Student Rule 7, or other reason deemed appropriate by the instructor.

Please refer to Student Rule 7 in its entirety for information about makeup work, including definitions, and related documentation and timelines.

Absences related to Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972 may necessitate a period of more than 30 days for make-up work, and the timeframe for make-up work should be agreed upon by the student and instructor" (Student Rule 7, Section 7.4.1).

"The instructor is under no obligation to provide an opportunity for the student to make up work missed because of an unexcused absence" (Student Rule 7, Section 7.4.2).

Students who request an excused absence are expected to uphold the Aggie Honor Code and Student Conduct Code. (See Student Rule 24.)

chunks.title: Academic Integrity Statement and Policy
chunks.content:
"An Aggie does not lie, cheat or steal, or tolerate those who do."

"Texas A&M University students are responsible for authenticating all work submitted to an instructor. If asked, students must be able to produce proof that the item submitted is indeed the work of that student. Students must keep appropriate records at all times. The inability to authenticate one's work, should the instructor request it, may be sufficient grounds to initiate an academic misconduct case" (Section 20.1.2.3, Student Rule 20).

Texas A&M at College Station
You can learn more about the Aggie Honor System Office Rules and Procedures, academic integrity, and your rights and responsibilities at aggiehonor.tamu.edu.

chunks.title: Notice of Nondiscrimination
chunks.content: Texas A&M University is committed to providing safe and non-discriminatory learning, living, and work environments for all members of the University community. The University provides equal opportunity to all employees, students, applicants for employment or admission, and the public regardless of race, color, sex (including pregnancy and related conditions), religion, national origin, age, disability, genetic information, or veteran status. Texas A&M University will promptly, thoroughly, and fairly investigate and resolve all complaints of discrimination, harassment (including sexual harassment), complicity and related retaliation based on a protected class in accordance with System Regulation 08.01.01, University Rule 08.01.01.M1, Standard Administrative Procedure (SAP) 08.01.01.M1.01, and applicable federal and state laws. In accordance with Title IX and its implementing regulations, Texas A&M does not discriminate on the basis of sex in any educational program or activity, including admissions and employment. The following person has been designated to handle inquiries and complaints regarding the non-discrimination policies: Jennifer M. Smith, TAMU Associate VP & Title IX Coordinator at YMCA Ste 108, College Station, TX 77843, 979-458-8407, or email civilrights@tamu.edu. For other reporting options, visit https://ocrcas.ed.gov/contact-ocr to locate the address and phone number of the office that serves your area, or call 1-800-421-3481.

chunks.title: Civil Rights, Free Speech, and Title IX Policies
chunks.content:
Texas A&M University is committed to fostering a learning environment that is safe and productive for all. University policies and federal and state laws prohibit discrimination and harassment based on an individual's race, color, sex, (including pregnancy and related conditions), religion, national origin, age, disability, genetic information, veteran status, or any other legally protected characteristic. This includes forms of sex-based violence, such as sexual assault, sexual harassment, sexual exploitation, dating/domestic violence, and stalking.

Students can report discrimination/harassment, access supportive resources, or learn more about their options for resolving complaints on the University's Civil Rights & Title IX webpage.

Students should be aware that all university employees (except medical or mental health providers) are mandatory reporters, which means that if they observe, experience or become aware of an incident that they reasonably believe to be discrimination/harassment alleged to have been committed by or against a person who was a student or employee at the time of the incident, the employee must report the incident to the university.

chunks.title: Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) Policy
chunks.content:
Texas A&M University is committed to providing equitable access to learning opportunities for all students. If you experience barriers to your education due to a disability or think you may have a disability, please contact the Disability Resources office on your campus (resources listed below) Disabilities may include, but are not limited to attentional, learning, mental health, sensory, physical, or chronic health conditions. All students are encouraged to discuss their disability related needs with Disability Resources and their instructors as soon as possible.

To request academic accommodations, contact the designated ADA office based on your location:
• Texas A&M University, College of Nursing, College of Dentistry, Irma Lerma Rangel College of Pharmacy College Station, College of Medicine, School of Public Health, Institute of Biosciences and Technology, EnMed Program, Bush School in Washington DC, Mays Business School – CityCentre, TAMU Engineering Academies, Texas A&M University Higher Education Center at McAllen and Texas A&M University at Galveston should contact Disability Resources at (979) 845-1637 or disability@tamu.edu.
• Texas A&M University School of Law should contact the Office of Student Affairs at (817) 212-4111 or law-disability@law.tamu.edu to request accommodations.
• Irma Lerma Rangel College of Pharmacy in Kingsville should contact the Disability Resource Center at Texas A&M University - Kingsville at (361) 593-3024 or drc.center@tamuk.edu to request accommodations.
• Texas A&M University College of Veterinary Medicine & Biomedical Sciences in Canyon should contact the Office of Student Accessibility at West Texas A&M University – Canyon at (806) 651-2335 or osa@wtamu.edu.
• Texas A&M University at Qatar (TAMUQ) should contact the campus psychologist, Dr. Steve Wilson +974-4423-0047 or stephen.wilson@qatar.tamu.edu.

If you are experiencing difficulties with your approved accommodations, contact the office responsible for approving your accommodations or the Texas A&M ADA Coordinator Julie Kuder at ADA.Coordinator@tamu.edu or (979) 458-8407.

chunks.title: Pregnancy Accommodations
chunks.content: Texas A&M provides reasonable accommodations to students due to pregnancy and/or related conditions, such as childbirth, recovery and lactation. Students should contact the University's Pregnancy Coordinator as soon as they become aware of the need for accommodation. Depending on the circumstances, accommodations could include extended time to complete assignments or exams, changes in course sequence, or modifications to the physical classroom environment. Texas A&M will also allow a voluntary leave of absence, ensure the availability of lactation space, and maintain grievance procedures to provide for the prompt and equitable resolution of complaints of sex discrimination. For information regarding pregnancy accommodations, email TIX.Pregnancy@tamu.edu.

chunks.title: Statement on Mental Health and Wellness
chunks.content:
Texas A&M University recognizes that mental health and wellness are critical factors influencing a student's academic success and overall wellbeing. Students are encouraged to engage in healthy self-care practices by utilizing the resources and services available through University Health Services on its mental health webpage. The TELUS Health Student Support app provides access to professional counseling in multiple languages anytime, anywhere by phone or chat, and the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline offers 24-hour emergency support at 988 or 988lifeline.org.

Students needing a listening ear can contact University Health Services (979.458.4584) 24-hour emergency help is also available through the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline (988) or at 988lifeline.org.

chunks.title: Statement on the Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA)
chunks.content:
FERPA is a federal law designed to protect the privacy of educational records by limiting access to these records, to establish the right of students to inspect and review their educational records and to provide guidelines for the correction of inaccurate and misleading data through informal and formal hearings. Currently enrolled students wishing to withhold any or all directory information items can do so within howdy.tamu.edu using the Directory Information Withholding Form. The complete FERPA Notice to Students and the student records policy is available on the Office of the Registrar webpage.

Items that can never be identified as public information are a student's social security number, citizenship, gender, grades, GPR or class schedule. All efforts will be made in this class to protect your privacy and to ensure confidential treatment of information associated with or generated by your participation in the class.

Directory items include name, UIN, local address, permanent address, email address, local telephone number, permanent telephone number, dates of attendance, program of study (college, major, campus), classification, previous institutions attended, degrees honors and awards received, participation in officially recognized activities and sports, medical residence location and medical residence specialization.

chunks.title: Campus-Specific Policies
chunks.content: NA

chunks.title: College and Department Policies
chunks.content: NA

chunks.title: Free Speech and Civil Discourse
chunks.content: Texas A&M recognizes that the pursuit of truth through open and robust discourse is critical to academic inquiry. However, as a community of scholars, the university has an aspirational expectation that such discourse will be conducted in accordance with Aggie Core Values. In this "marketplace of ideas," we encourage civil dialogue creating an environment that allows individuals to express their ideas and to have their ideas challenged in respectful and responsible ways. Students can learn more about Freedom of Expression and Free Speech on the University's website about the First Amendment.

chunks.title: Artificial Intelligence Statements
chunks.content: There are two recommended syllabus statement options for instructions considering adding a statement about the use of Artificial Intelligence in their course. These are located on the Teach with AI webpage hosted at ai.tamu.edu. Faculty are strongly encouraged to have a written policy on AI use in their course to avoid any confusion regarding what is or is not permitted.
